title: Parameterized Quantum Circuits as Feature Maps: Representation Quality and Readout Effects in Multispectral Land-Cover Classification
authors: Ralntion Komini, Aikaterini Mandilara, Georgios Maragkopoulos, Dimitris Syvridis
abstract: We investigate variational quantum classifiers (VQCs) for land-cover classification from multispectral satellite imagery, adopting a feature-map perspective in which the quantum circuit defines a nonlinear data embedding while the readout determines how this representation is exploited. Using the EuroSAT-MS dataset, we perform a systematic one-vs-one evaluation across all class pairs under a controlled experimental protocol, comparing classical baselines (logistic regression, SVMs, neural networks) with VQCs employing both linear readout and quantum-kernel SVM strategies. Our results show that, while VQCs with linear readout do not outperform strong classical baselines such as RBF-SVM, the same trained quantum feature map can significantly improve performance when reused within a kernel-based decision framework. A qubit-count sweep further reveals saturation effects consistent with the mismatch between exponential Hilbert space dimension and linear parameter scaling. Overall, our findings highlight that the effectiveness of quantum models depends critically on the interplay between representation and readout, and that meaningful gains may arise from combining learned quantum feature maps with classical decision mechanisms rather than seeking direct replacement of classical models.
summary: This paper explores using parameterized quantum circuits as learned nonlinear feature maps for satellite imagery classification. It demonstrates that while standard quantum classifiers struggle to beat classical models, the learned quantum representation is highly effective when integrated into a classical kernel-based SVM. The study also identifies a saturation effect in performance as the number of qubits increases. Ultimately, it suggests that the synergy between quantum representations and classical decision mechanisms is key to potential quantum utility.
main_problem: Investigating the effectiveness of Variational Quantum Classifiers (VQCs) as nonlinear feature maps for multispectral land-cover classification and determining how the choice of readout mechanism affects performance.
main_result: While VQCs with linear readout do not outperform classical RBF-SVMs, the trained quantum feature map significantly improves performance when used within a kernel-based decision framework (SVM-QK).
method: The authors perform a systematic one-vs-one evaluation on the EuroSAT-MS dataset, comparing VQCs and Quantum Kernel SVMs against classical baselines like Logistic Regression, SVMs, and Neural Networks using noiseless state-vector simulations.
model_or_system: A parameterized quantum circuit (PQC) utilizing a data re-uploading architecture with 1 to 7 qubits, featuring an initialization block, data-dependent rotations, and alternating entangling layers.
key_observables: Pauli-Z expectation values used to construct classical feature vectors.
important_parameters: Number of qubits (1-7), number of trainable parameters, and PCA-reduced input dimensions.
main_assumptions: The study assumes noiseless state-vector simulation, omitting hardware noise, finite-shot effects, and full spatial image structures.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the VQC architecture; Figure 2 details the 4-qubit PQC structure; Figure 3 compares per-class accuracy of VQC, NN, and LogReg; Figure 4 compares different SVM kernels; Figure 5 compares VQC linear head vs. SVM-QK; Figure 6 shows accuracy scaling with qubit count.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the VQC architecture and problem, describes the PQC model and data preprocessing, presents a comparative analysis of different readout strategies and classical baselines, evaluates scalability via a qubit-count sweep, and concludes with limitations and future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
